chemicals.concentration_notes: The toxic cyanogenic glycosides are generally concentrated within the leaves of the plant and the seeds of its fruit2. Severe toxicity is relatively uncommon unless a large amount of the plant is ingested2. Furthermore, the seeds must be physically compromised to pose a danger; if a cat swallows a seed completely whole without chewing it, the cyanide is not released and toxicity is not expected
chemicals.description: in cats: When a cat chews, grinds, or crushes the plant material, the cyanogenic glycosides are altered and release toxic cyanide into the body2. Once absorbed, the cyanide binds to ferric iron (Fe 3+ ), specifically inhibiting the mitochondrial enzyme cytochrome c oxidase24. This interference halts cellular respiration, completely preventing the cells from utilizing oxygen and resulting in rapid,
chemicals.name: Cyanogenic glycosides, which metabolize and break down to release cyanide (specifically hydrogen cyanide)

description: Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus spp.) is a shrub or small tree in the rose family that is toxic to cats due to cyanogenic glycosides — primarily prunasin — found in its leaves. When the leaves are chewed or wilted, prunasin is enzymatically converted to hydrogen cyanide (HCN), which blocks cellular respiration. Wilted or dried leaves are particularly dangerous as the enzymatic conversion proceeds more readily.
emergencyNote: Antidote Administration (Sodium Nitrite, Sodium Thiosulfate, or Hydroxycobalamin)
name: Mountain Mahogany
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: prunasin (cyanogenic glycoside); wilted leaves most dangerous
Mechanism: prunasin releases HCN via beta-glucosidase, inhibiting cytochrome c oxidase and halting cellular respiration
Symptoms: rapid breathing, weakness, dilated pupils, drooling; severe cases cause seizures, coma, and respiratory failure
Wilted or fallen leaves pose a higher risk than fresh leaves due to accelerated cyanogenic conversion
Seek veterinary care immediately if ingestion is suspected — cyanide toxicity can progress within minutes

symptoms.name: Respiratory distress (panting, gasping for breath, or labored breathing)
symptoms.notes: This is typically one of the most prominent signs of cyanide poisoning, occurring because the toxin severely hampers the body's ability to utilize oxygen at the cellular level45. 2. Unusual Gum Color (Bright Red Gums)

symptoms.name: Unusually red or pink gums (bright red mucous membranes)
symptoms.notes: This distinct symptom happens because the cells are unable to extract oxygen from the blood. The blood remains fully oxygenated, causing the gums and mucous membranes to appear bright red while the body's tissues are essentially suffocating35. 3. Dilated Pupils

symptoms.name: Dilated pupils
symptoms.notes: Abnormally large pupils act as an indicator of severe systemic stress and neurological impairment from the rapid lack of usable oxygen3.... 4. Excessive Drooling

symptoms.name: Excessive drooling (hypersalivation)
symptoms.notes: Salivating much more than usual can be one of the earlier, acute signs of toxic exposure before severe neurological decline sets in5. 5. Shock, Seizures, and Coma

symptoms.name: Shock, seizures (convulsions), loss of consciousness, and coma
symptoms.notes: These extreme neurological and cardiovascular symptoms manifest as the oxygen deprivation peaks. Cyanide poisoning acts very quickly; if prompt veterinary attention (which may include oxygen therapy and specific antidotes) is not provided, it rapidly progresses to death

treatments.description: The veterinarian administers specific antidotes to neutralize the cyanide in the bloodstream. This typically involves using sodium nitrite (which binds with cyanide to form cyanomethemoglobin) followed by sodium thiosulfate (which converts the toxin into water-soluble thiocyanate that is safely excreted in the urine)4. Alternatively, the vet may use hydroxycobalamin, which combines with cyanide to
treatments.name: Antidote Administration (Sodium Nitrite, Sodium Thiosulfate, or Hydroxycobalamin)
treatments.notes: This is the most urgent life-saving intervention for a cat showing clinical signs of large-dose poisoning (such as respiratory distress, cherry-red blood/gums, seizures, or collapse)3. Less severe cases might be successfully treated using sodium thiosulfate alone4. 2. Immediate Gastrointestinal Deco

treatments.description: If the cat was exposed recently and is not yet showing neurological or respiratory symptoms, the vet will induce vomiting to clear the stomach of the plant material. This is followed by administering a single dose of activated charcoal alongside a cathartic to bind to any unabsorbed toxins in the digestive tract
treatments.name: Emesis Induction and Activated Charcoal
treatments.notes: Decontamination should only be performed on asymptomatic patients. If the cat only ingested a very small amount of the plant, severe toxicity is less likely, and the cat might ultimately only require minimal gastrointestinal support4. 3. Systemic Metabolic Support and Monitoring

treatments.description: The veterinary team will routinely monitor the cat's venous blood gas levels to check for severe metabolic acidosis, which is a dangerous secondary effect of cyanide halting cellular respiration. If acidosis is present, it is corrected by intravenously administering sodium bicarbonate
treatments.name: Blood Gas Monitoring and Sodium Bicarbonate Therapy
treatments.notes: Alongside blood gas monitoring, the veterinarian may also draw a whole blood cyanide level to definitively confirm the diagnosis
